name: pyro4
documentation:
Pyro means PYthon Remote Objects.
It is a library that enables you to build applications in which
objects can talk to eachother over the network, with minimal programming effort.
You can just use normal Python method calls, with almost every possible parameter
and return value type, and Pyro takes care of locating the right object on the right
computer to execute the method. It is designed to be very easy to use, and to
generally stay out of your way. But it also provides a set of powerful features that
enables you to build distributed applications rapidly and effortlessly.
Pyro is a pure Python library and runs on many different platforms and Python versions.

The source code repository is on Github: https://github.com/irmen/Pyro4

The documentation can be found here: http://pyro4.readthedocs.io

modules.name: Pyro4.errors
documentation:
Definition of the various exceptions that are used in Pyro.

Pyro - Python Remote Objects.  Copyright by Irmen de Jong (irmen@razorvine.net).

classes.name: Pyro4.errors.PyroError
documentation: Generic base of all Pyro-specific errors.

classes.name: Pyro4.errors.CommunicationError
documentation: Base class for the errors related to network communication problems.

classes.name: Pyro4.errors.ConnectionClosedError
documentation: The connection was unexpectedly closed.

classes.name: Pyro4.errors.TimeoutError
documentation:
A call could not be completed within the set timeout period,
or the network caused a timeout.

classes.name: Pyro4.errors.ProtocolError
documentation: Pyro received a message that didn't match the active Pyro network protocol, or there was a protocol related error.

classes.name: Pyro4.errors.MessageTooLargeError
documentation: Pyro received a message or was trying to send a message that exceeds the maximum message size as configured.

classes.name: Pyro4.errors.NamingError
documentation: There was a problem related to the name server or object names.

classes.name: Pyro4.errors.DaemonError
documentation: The Daemon encountered a problem.

classes.name: Pyro4.errors.SecurityError
documentation: A security related error occurred.

classes.name: Pyro4.errors.SerializeError
documentation: Something went wrong while (de)serializing data.

modules.name: Pyro4.futures
documentation:
Support for Futures (asynchronously executed callables).
If you're using Python 3.2 or newer, also see
http://docs.python.org/3/library/concurrent.futures.html#future-objects

Pyro - Python Remote Objects.  Copyright by Irmen de Jong (irmen@razorvine.net).

classes.name: Pyro4.futures.Future

methods.name: Pyro4.futures.Future.__call__
documentation:
Start the future call with the provided arguments.
Control flow returns immediately, with a FutureResult object.

methods.name: Pyro4.futures.Future.delay
documentation:
Delay the evaluation of the future for the given number of seconds.
Return True if successful otherwise False if the future has already been evaluated.

methods.name: Pyro4.futures.Future.cancel
documentation:
Cancels the execution of the future altogether.
If the execution hasn't been started yet, the cancellation is successful and returns True.
Otherwise, it failed and returns False.

methods.name: Pyro4.futures.Future.then
documentation:
Add a callable to the call chain, to be invoked when the results become available.
The result of the current call will be used as the first argument for the next call.
Optional extra arguments can be provided in args and kwargs.
Returns self so you can easily chain then() calls.

methods.name: Pyro4.futures.Future.iferror
documentation:
Specify the exception handler to be invoked (with the exception object as only
argument) when calculating the result raises an exception.
If no exception handler is set, any exception raised in the asynchronous call will be silently ignored.
Returns self so you can easily chain other calls.

Holds a callable that will be executed asynchronously and provide its
result value some time in the future.
This is a more general implementation than the AsyncRemoteMethod, which
only works with Pyro proxies (and provides a bit different syntax).
This class has a few extra features as well (delay, canceling).

classes.name: Pyro4.futures.FutureResult

methods.name: Pyro4.futures.FutureResult.wait
documentation:
Wait for the result to become available, with optional timeout (in seconds).
Returns True if the result is ready, or False if it still isn't ready.

methods.name: Pyro4.futures.FutureResult.ready
documentation: Boolean that contains the readiness of the asynchronous result

methods.name: Pyro4.futures.FutureResult.then
documentation:
Add a callable to the call chain, to be invoked when the results become available.
The result of the current call will be used as the first argument for the next call.
Optional extra arguments can be provided in args and kwargs.
Returns self so you can easily chain then() calls.

methods.name: Pyro4.futures.FutureResult.iferror
documentation:
Specify the exception handler to be invoked (with the exception object as only
argument) when asking for the result raises an exception.
If no exception handler is set, any exception result will be silently ignored (unless
you explicitly ask for the value). Returns self so you can easily chain other calls.

The result object for asynchronous Pyro calls.
Unfortunatley it should be similar to the more general Future class but
it is still somewhat limited (no delay, no canceling).

classes.name: Pyro4.futures._ExceptionWrapper

methods.name: Pyro4.futures._ExceptionWrapper.__serialized_dict__
documentation: serialized form as a dictionary

Class that wraps a remote exception. If this is returned, Pyro will
re-throw the exception on the receiving side. Usually this is taken care of
by a special response message flag, but in the case of batched calls this
flag is useless and another mechanism was needed.

modules.name: Pyro4.test
documentation: just to make this a package.

modules.name: Pyro4.test.echoserver
documentation:
Echo server for test purposes.
This is usually invoked by starting this module as a script:

> {command}`python -m Pyro4.test.echoserver`
> or simply: {command}`pyro4-test-echoserver`

It is also possible to use the {class}`EchoServer` in user code
but that is not terribly useful.

Pyro - Python Remote Objects.  Copyright by Irmen de Jong (<mailto:irmen@razorvine.net>).

classes.name: Pyro4.test.echoserver.EchoServer

methods.name: Pyro4.test.echoserver.EchoServer.echo
documentation: return the message

methods.name: Pyro4.test.echoserver.EchoServer.error
documentation: generates a simple exception without text

methods.name: Pyro4.test.echoserver.EchoServer.error_with_text
documentation: generates a simple exception with message

methods.name: Pyro4.test.echoserver.EchoServer.oneway_echo
documentation: just like echo, but oneway; the client won't wait for response

methods.name: Pyro4.test.echoserver.EchoServer.slow
documentation: returns (and prints) a message after a certain delay

methods.name: Pyro4.test.echoserver.EchoServer.generator
documentation: a generator function that returns some elements on demand

methods.name: Pyro4.test.echoserver.EchoServer.oneway_slow
documentation: prints a message after a certain delay, and returns; but the client won't wait for it

methods.name: Pyro4.test.echoserver.EchoServer._private
documentation: a 'private' method that should not be accessible

methods.name: Pyro4.test.echoserver.EchoServer.__private
documentation: another 'private' method that should not be accessible

methods.name: Pyro4.test.echoserver.EchoServer.__dunder__
documentation: a double underscore method that should be accessible normally

methods.name: Pyro4.test.echoserver.EchoServer.shutdown
documentation: called to signal the echo server to shut down

The echo server object that is provided as a Pyro object by this module.
If its {attr}`verbose` attribute is set to `True`, it will print messages as it receives calls.

modules.name: Pyro4.configuration
documentation:
Configuration settings.

Pyro - Python Remote Objects.  Copyright by Irmen de Jong (irmen@razorvine.net).

functions.name: Pyro4.configuration.main
documentation: entrypoint from script

methods.name: Pyro4.configuration.Configuration.reset
documentation:
Set default config items.
If useenvironment is False, won't read environment variables settings (useful if you can't trust your env).

methods.name: Pyro4.configuration.Configuration.asDict
documentation: returns the current config as a regular dictionary

methods.name: Pyro4.configuration.Configuration.parseAddressesString
documentation:
Parses the addresses string which contains one or more ip addresses separated by a comma.
Returns a sequence of these addresses. '' is replaced by the empty string.

modules.name: Pyro4.naming_storage
documentation:
Name Server persistent storage implementations.

Pyro - Python Remote Objects.  Copyright by Irmen de Jong (irmen@razorvine.net).

classes.name: Pyro4.naming_storage.SqlStorage
documentation:
Sqlite-based storage.
It is just a single (name,uri) table for the names and another table for the metadata.
Sqlite db connection objects aren't thread-safe, so a new connection is created in every method.

classes.name: Pyro4.naming_storage.DbmStorage
documentation:
Storage implementation that uses a persistent dbm file.
Because dbm only supports strings as key/value, we encode/decode them in utf-8.
Dbm files cannot be accessed concurrently, so a strict concurrency model
is used where only one operation is processed at the same time
(this is very slow when compared to the in-memory storage)
DbmStorage does NOT support storing metadata! It only accepts empty metadata,
and always returns empty metadata.

Pyro - Python Remote Objects.  Copyright by Irmen de Jong (irmen@razorvine.net).

modules.name: Pyro4.socketserver.threadpoolserver
documentation:
Socket server based on a worker thread pool. Doesn't use select.

Uses a single worker thread per client connection.

Pyro - Python Remote Objects.  Copyright by Irmen de Jong (irmen@razorvine.net).

classes.name: Pyro4.socketserver.threadpoolserver.ClientConnectionJob
documentation:
Takes care of a single client connection and all requests
that may arrive during its life span.

classes.name: Pyro4.socketserver.threadpoolserver.SocketServer_Threadpool

methods.name: Pyro4.socketserver.threadpoolserver.SocketServer_Threadpool.events

transport server for socket connections, worker thread pool version.

Definitions of various hard coded constants.

Pyro - Python Remote Objects.  Copyright by Irmen de Jong (irmen@razorvine.net).

modules.name: Pyro4.socketserver.threadpool
documentation:
Thread pool job processor with variable number of worker threads (between max/min amount).

Pyro - Python Remote Objects.  Copyright by Irmen de Jong (irmen@razorvine.net).

classes.name: Pyro4.socketserver.threadpool.Pool
documentation:
A job processing pool that is using a pool of worker threads.
The amount of worker threads in the pool is configurable and scales between min/max size.

modules.name: Pyro4.socketserver.multiplexserver
documentation:
Socket server based on socket multiplexing. Doesn't use threads.
Uses the best available selector (kqueue, poll, select).

Pyro - Python Remote Objects.  Copyright by Irmen de Jong (irmen@razorvine.net).

classes.name: Pyro4.socketserver.multiplexserver.SocketServer_Multiplex

methods.name: Pyro4.socketserver.multiplexserver.SocketServer_Multiplex.events
documentation: handle events that occur on one of the sockets of this server

methods.name: Pyro4.socketserver.multiplexserver.SocketServer_Multiplex.wakeup
documentation: bit of a hack to trigger a blocking server to get out of the loop, useful at clean shutdowns

methods.name: Pyro4.socketserver.multiplexserver.SocketServer_Multiplex.handleRequest
documentation: Handles a single connection request event and returns if the connection is still active
Multiplexed transport server for socket connections (uses select, poll, kqueue, ...)

modules.name: Pyro4
documentation:
Pyro package. Some generic init stuff to set up logging etc.

Pyro - Python Remote Objects.  Copyright by Irmen de Jong (irmen@razorvine.net).

functions.name: Pyro4._configLogging
documentation:
Do some basic config of the logging module at package import time.
The configuring is done only if the PYRO_LOGLEVEL env var is set.
If you want to use your own logging config, make sure you do
that before any Pyro imports. Then Pyro will skip the autoconfig.
Set the env var PYRO_LOGFILE to change the name of the autoconfigured
log file (default is pyro.log in the current dir). Use '{stderr}' to
make the log go to the standard error output.

modules.name: Pyro4.message
documentation:
The pyro wire protocol message.

Pyro - Python Remote Objects.  Copyright by Irmen de Jong (irmen@razorvine.net).

classes.name: Pyro4.message.Message

methods.name: Pyro4.message.Message.to_bytes
documentation: creates a byte stream containing the header followed by annotations (if any) followed by the data

methods.name: Pyro4.message.Message.send
documentation: send the message as bytes over the connection
Note: this 'chunked' way of sending is not used because it triggers Nagle's algorithm
on some systems (linux). This causes big delays, unless you change the socket option
TCP_NODELAY to disable the algorithm. What also works, is sending all the message bytes
in one go: connection.send(message.to_bytes()). This is what Pyro does.

methods.name: Pyro4.message.Message.from_header
documentation: Parses a message header. Does not yet process the annotations chunks and message data.

methods.name: Pyro4.message.Message.recv
documentation:
Receives a pyro message from a given connection.
Accepts the given message types (None=any, or pass a sequence).
Also reads annotation chunks and the actual payload data.
Validates a HMAC chunk if present.

methods.name: Pyro4.message.Message.hmac
documentation: returns the hmac of the data and the annotation chunk values (except HMAC chunk itself)

methods.name: Pyro4.message.Message.ping
documentation: Convenience method to send a 'ping' message and wait for the 'pong' response

methods.name: Pyro4.message.Message.decompress_if_needed
documentation: Decompress the message data if it is compressed.

Pyro write protocol message.

Wire messages contains of a fixed size header, an optional set of annotation chunks,
and then the payload data. This class doesn't deal with the payload data:
(de)serialization and handling of that data is done elsewhere.
Annotation chunks are only parsed, except the 'HMAC' chunk: that is created
and validated because it is used as a message digest.

The header format is:

```
4   id ('PYRO')
2   protocol version
2   message type
2   message flags
2   sequence number
4   data length   (i.e. 2 Gb data size limitation)
2   data serialization format (serializer id)
2   annotations length (total of all chunks, 0 if no annotation chunks present)
2   (reserved)
2   checksum
```

After the header, zero or more annotation chunks may follow, of the format:

```
4   id (ASCII)
2   chunk length
x   annotation chunk databytes
```

After that, the actual payload data bytes follow.

The sequencenumber is used to check if response messages correspond to the
actual request message. This prevents the situation where Pyro would perhaps return
the response data from another remote call (which would not result in an error otherwise!)
This could happen for instance if the socket data stream gets out of sync, perhaps due To
some form of signal that interrupts I/O.

The header checksum is a simple sum of the header fields to make reasonably sure
that we are dealing with an actual correct PYRO protocol header and not some random
data that happens to start with the 'PYRO' protocol identifier.

Pyro now uses two annotation chunks that you should not touch yourself:
'HMAC'  contains the hmac digest of the message data bytes and
all of the annotation chunk data bytes (except those of the HMAC chunk itself).
'CORR'  contains the correlation id (guid bytes)
Other chunk names are free to use for custom purposes, but Pyro has the right
to reserve more of them for internal use in the future.

modules.name: Pyro4.nsc
documentation:
Name server control tool.

Pyro - Python Remote Objects.  Copyright by Irmen de Jong (irmen@razorvine.net).

modules.name: Pyro4.naming
documentation:
Name Server and helper functions.

Pyro - Python Remote Objects.  Copyright by Irmen de Jong (irmen@razorvine.net).

functions.name: Pyro4.naming.startNSloop
documentation: utility function that starts a new Name server and enters its requestloop.

functions.name: Pyro4.naming.startNS
documentation:
utility fuction to quickly get a Name server daemon to be used in your own event loops.
Returns (nameserverUri, nameserverDaemon, broadcastServer).

functions.name: Pyro4.naming.type_meta
documentation: extracts type metadata from the given class or object, can be used as Name server metadata.

classes.name: Pyro4.naming.MemoryStorage
documentation:
Storage implementation that is just an in-memory dict.
(because it inherits from dict it is automatically a collections.MutableMapping)
Stopping the nameserver will make the server instantly forget about everything.

classes.name: Pyro4.naming.NameServer

methods.name: Pyro4.naming.NameServer.count
documentation: Returns the number of name registrations.

methods.name: Pyro4.naming.NameServer.lookup
documentation:
Lookup the given name, returns an URI if found.
Returns tuple (uri, metadata) if return_metadata is True.

methods.name: Pyro4.naming.NameServer.register
documentation:
Register a name with an URI. If safe is true, name cannot be registered twice.
The uri can be a string or an URI object. Metadata must be None, or a collection of strings.

methods.name: Pyro4.naming.NameServer.set_metadata
documentation: update the metadata for an existing registration

methods.name: Pyro4.naming.NameServer.remove
documentation: Remove a registration. returns the number of items removed.

methods.name: Pyro4.naming.NameServer.list
documentation:
Retrieve the registered items as a dictionary name-to-URI. The URIs
in the resulting dict are strings, not URI objects.
You can filter by prefix or by regex or by metadata subset (separately)
noinspection PyNoneFunctionAssignment

methods.name: Pyro4.naming.NameServer.ping
documentation: A simple test method to check if the name server is running correctly.

Pyro name server. Provides a simple flat name space to map logical object names to Pyro URIs.
Default storage is done in an in-memory dictionary. You can provide custom storage types.

classes.name: Pyro4.naming.NameServerDaemon
documentation: Daemon that contains the Name Server.

classes.name: Pyro4.naming.AutoCleaner

class_variables.name: Pyro4.naming.AutoCleaner.override_autoclean_min
documentation: only for unit test purposes

Takes care of checking every registration in the name server.
If it cannot be contacted anymore, it will be removed after ~20 seconds.

methods.name: Pyro4.naming.BroadcastServer.runInThread
documentation: Run the broadcast server loop in its own thread.

methods.name: Pyro4.naming.BroadcastServer.TransportServerAdapter.__init__
documentation: this adapter is used to be able to pass the BroadcastServer to Daemon.combine() to integrate the event loops.

modules.name: Pyro4.utils.flameserver
documentation:
Pyro FLAME:  Foreign Location Automatic Module Exposer.
Easy but potentially very dangerous way of exposing remote modules and builtins.
This is the commandline server.

You can start this module as a script from the command line, to easily get a
flame server running:

> {command}`python -m Pyro4.utils.flameserver`
> or simply: {command}`pyro4-flameserver`

You have to explicitly enable Flame first though by setting the FLAME_ENABLED config item.

Pyro - Python Remote Objects.  Copyright by Irmen de Jong (<mailto:irmen@razorvine.net>).

modules.name: Pyro4.socketutil
documentation:
Low level socket utilities.

Pyro - Python Remote Objects.  Copyright by Irmen de Jong (irmen@razorvine.net).

functions.name: Pyro4.socketutil.getIpVersion
documentation:
Determine what the IP version is of the given hostname or ip address (4 or 6).
First, it resolves the hostname or address to get an IP address.
Then, if the resolved IP contains a ':' it is considered to be an ipv6 address,
and if it contains a '.', it is ipv4.

functions.name: Pyro4.socketutil.getIpAddress
documentation:
Returns the IP address for the given host. If you enable the workaround,
it will use a little hack if the ip address is found to be the loopback address.
The hack tries to discover an externally visible ip address instead (this only works for ipv4 addresses).
Set ipVersion=6 to return ipv6 addresses, 4 to return ipv4, 0 to let OS choose the best one or None to use config.PREFER_IP_VERSION.

functions.name: Pyro4.socketutil.getInterfaceAddress
documentation: tries to find the ip address of the interface that connects to the given host's address

functions.name: Pyro4.socketutil.receiveData
documentation:
Retrieve a given number of bytes from a socket.
It is expected the socket is able to supply that number of bytes.
If it isn't, an exception is raised (you will not get a zero length result
or a result that is smaller than what you asked for). The partial data that
has been received however is stored in the 'partialData' attribute of
the exception object.

functions.name: Pyro4.socketutil.sendData
documentation:
Send some data over a socket.
Some systems have problems with `sendall()` when the socket is in non-blocking mode.
For instance, Mac OS X seems to be happy to throw EAGAIN errors too often.
This function falls back to using a regular send loop if needed.

functions.name: Pyro4.socketutil.createSocket
documentation:
Create a socket. Default socket options are keepalive and IPv4 family, and nodelay (nagle disabled).
If 'bind' or 'connect' is a string, it is assumed a Unix domain socket is requested.
Otherwise, a normal tcp/ip socket is used.
Set ipv6=True to create an IPv6 socket rather than IPv4.
Set ipv6=None to use the PREFER_IP_VERSION config setting.

functions.name: Pyro4.socketutil.createBroadcastSocket
documentation:
Create a udp broadcast socket.
Set ipv6=True to create an IPv6 socket rather than IPv4.
Set ipv6=None to use the PREFER_IP_VERSION config setting.

functions.name: Pyro4.socketutil.setReuseAddr
documentation: sets the SO_REUSEADDR option on the socket, if possible.

functions.name: Pyro4.socketutil.setNoDelay
documentation: sets the TCP_NODELAY option on the socket (to disable Nagle's algorithm), if possible.

functions.name: Pyro4.socketutil.setKeepalive
documentation: sets the SO_KEEPALIVE option on the socket, if possible.

functions.name: Pyro4.socketutil.setNoInherit
documentation: Mark the given socket fd as non-inheritable to child processes

functions.name: Pyro4.socketutil.setNoInherit
documentation: Mark the given socket fd as non-inheritable to child processes

functions.name: Pyro4.socketutil.setNoInherit
documentation: Mark the given socket fd as non-inheritable to child processes (dummy)
nothing available, define a dummy function

functions.name: Pyro4.socketutil.findProbablyUnusedPort
documentation:
Returns an unused port that should be suitable for binding (likely, but not guaranteed).
This code is copied from the stdlib's test.test_support module.

functions.name: Pyro4.socketutil.bindOnUnusedPort
documentation:
Bind the socket to a free port and return the port number.
This code is based on the code in the stdlib's test.test_support module.

functions.name: Pyro4.socketutil.interruptSocket
documentation: bit of a hack to trigger a blocking server to get out of the loop, useful at clean shutdowns

functions.name: Pyro4.socketutil.getSSLcontext
documentation: creates an SSL context and caches it, so you have to set the parameters correctly before doing anything

variables.name: Pyro4.socketutil._SetHandleInformation.restype
documentation: don't need this, but might as well

classes.name: Pyro4.socketutil.SocketConnection
documentation: A wrapper class for plain sockets, containing various methods such as {meth}`send` and {meth}`recv`

modules.name: Pyro4.utils
documentation: just to make this a package.

modules.name: Pyro4.util
documentation:
Miscellaneous utilities, and serializers.

Pyro - Python Remote Objects.  Copyright by Irmen de Jong (irmen@razorvine.net).

functions.name: Pyro4.util.getPyroTraceback
documentation:
Returns a list of strings that form the traceback information of a
Pyro exception. Any remote Pyro exception information is included.
Traceback information is automatically obtained via `sys.exc_info()` if
you do not supply the objects yourself.

functions.name: Pyro4.util.formatTraceback
documentation:
Formats an exception traceback. If you ask for detailed formatting,
the result will contain info on the variables in each stack frame.
You don't have to provide the exception info objects, if you omit them,
this function will obtain them itself using `sys.exc_info()`.

functions.name: Pyro4.util.getAttribute
documentation:
Resolves an attribute name to an object.  Raises
an AttributeError if any attribute in the chain starts with a '`_`'.
Doesn't resolve a dotted name, because that is a security vulnerability.
It treats it as a single attribute name (and the lookup will likely fail).

functions.name: Pyro4.util.excepthook
documentation: An exception hook you can use for `sys.excepthook`, to automatically print remote Pyro tracebacks

functions.name: Pyro4.util.fixIronPythonExceptionForPickle
documentation:
Function to hack around a bug in IronPython where it doesn't pickle
exception attributes. We piggyback them into the exception's args.
Bug report is at https://github.com/IronLanguages/main/issues/943
Bug is still present in Ironpython 2.7.7

functions.name: Pyro4.util.reset_exposed_members
documentation: Delete any cached exposed members forcing recalculation on next request

functions.name: Pyro4.util.get_exposed_members
documentation:
Return public and exposed members of the given object's class.
You can also provide a class directly.
Private members are ignored no matter what (names starting with underscore).
If only_exposed is True, only members tagged with the @expose decorator are
returned. If it is False, all public members are returned.
The return value consists of the exposed methods, exposed attributes, and methods
tagged as @oneway.
(All this is used as meta data that Pyro sends to the proxy if it asks for it)
as_lists is meant for python 2 compatibility.

functions.name: Pyro4.util.get_exposed_property_value
documentation:
Return the value of an @exposed @property.
If the requested property is not a @property or not exposed,
an AttributeError is raised instead.

functions.name: Pyro4.util.set_exposed_property_value
documentation:
Sets the value of an @exposed @property.
If the requested property is not a @property or not exposed,
an AttributeError is raised instead.

functions.name: Pyro4.util.is_private_attribute
documentation: returns if the attribute name is to be considered private or not.

classes.name: Pyro4.util.SerializerBase

methods.name: Pyro4.util.SerializerBase.serializeData
documentation:
Serialize the given data object, try to compress if told so.
Returns a tuple of the serialized data (bytes) and a bool indicating if it is compressed or not.

methods.name: Pyro4.util.SerializerBase.deserializeData
documentation: Deserializes the given data (bytes). Set compressed to True to decompress the data first.

methods.name: Pyro4.util.SerializerBase.serializeCall
documentation:
Serialize the given method call parameters, try to compress if told so.
Returns a tuple of the serialized data and a bool indicating if it is compressed or not.

methods.name: Pyro4.util.SerializerBase.deserializeCall
documentation:
Deserializes the given call data back to (object, method, vargs, kwargs) tuple.
Set compressed to True to decompress the data first.

methods.name: Pyro4.util.SerializerBase.register_class_to_dict
documentation:
Registers a custom function that returns a dict representation of objects of the given class.
The function is called with a single parameter; the object to be converted to a dict.

methods.name: Pyro4.util.SerializerBase.unregister_class_to_dict
documentation:
Removes the to-dict conversion function registered for the given class. Objects of the class
will be serialized by the default mechanism again.

methods.name: Pyro4.util.SerializerBase.register_dict_to_class
documentation:
Registers a custom converter function that creates objects from a dict with the given classname tag in it.
The function is called with two parameters: the classname and the dictionary to convert to an instance of the class.

This mechanism is not used for the pickle serializer.

methods.name: Pyro4.util.SerializerBase.unregister_dict_to_class
documentation:
Removes the converter registered for the given classname. Dicts with that classname tag
will be deserialized by the default mechanism again.

This mechanism is not used for the pickle serializer.

methods.name: Pyro4.util.SerializerBase.class_to_dict
documentation:
Convert a non-serializable object to a dict. Partly borrowed from serpent.
Not used for the pickle serializer.

methods.name: Pyro4.util.SerializerBase.dict_to_class
documentation:
Recreate an object out of a dict containing the class name and the attributes.
Only a fixed set of classes are recognized.
Not used for the pickle serializer.

methods.name: Pyro4.util.SerializerBase.__eq__
documentation: this equality method is only to support the unit tests of this class
Base class for (de)serializer implementations (which must be thread safe)

classes.name: Pyro4.util.PickleSerializer

class_variables.name: Pyro4.util.PickleSerializer.serializer_id
documentation: never change this

A (de)serializer that wraps the Pickle serialization protocol.
It can optionally compress the serialized data, and is thread safe.

classes.name: Pyro4.util.CloudpickleSerializer

class_variables.name: Pyro4.util.CloudpickleSerializer.serializer_id
documentation: never change this

A (de)serializer that wraps the Cloudpickle serialization protocol.
It can optionally compress the serialized data, and is thread safe.

classes.name: Pyro4.util.DillSerializer

class_variables.name: Pyro4.util.DillSerializer.serializer_id
documentation: never change this

A (de)serializer that wraps the Dill serialization protocol.
It can optionally compress the serialized data, and is thread safe.

classes.name: Pyro4.util.MarshalSerializer

class_variables.name: Pyro4.util.MarshalSerializer.serializer_id
documentation: never change this
(de)serializer that wraps the marshal serialization protocol.

classes.name: Pyro4.util.SerpentSerializer

class_variables.name: Pyro4.util.SerpentSerializer.serializer_id
documentation: never change this
(de)serializer that wraps the serpent serialization protocol.

classes.name: Pyro4.util.JsonSerializer

(de)serializer that wraps the json serialization protocol.

modules.name: Pyro4.utils.httpgateway
documentation:
HTTP gateway: connects the web browser's world of javascript+http and Pyro.
Creates a stateless HTTP server that essentially is a proxy for the Pyro objects behind it.
It exposes the Pyro objects through a HTTP interface and uses the JSON serializer,
so that you can immediately process the response data in the browser.

You can start this module as a script from the command line, to easily get a
http gateway server running:

> {command}`python -m Pyro4.utils.httpgateway`
> or simply: {command}`pyro4-httpgateway`

It is also possible to import the 'pyro_app' function and stick that into a WSGI
server of your choice, to have more control.

The javascript code in the web page of the gateway server works with the same-origin
browser policy because it is served by the gateway itself. If you want to access it
from scripts in different sites, you have to work around this or embed the gateway app
in your site. Non-browser clients that access the http api have no problems.
See the `http` example for two of such clients (node.js and python).

Pyro - Python Remote Objects.  Copyright by Irmen de Jong (<mailto:irmen@razorvine.net>).

functions.name: Pyro4.utils.httpgateway.invalid_request
documentation: Called if invalid http method.

functions.name: Pyro4.utils.httpgateway.not_found
documentation: Called if Url not found.

functions.name: Pyro4.utils.httpgateway.redirect
documentation: Called to do a redirect

functions.name: Pyro4.utils.httpgateway.pyro_app
documentation:
The WSGI app function that is used to process the requests.
You can stick this into a wsgi server of your choice, or use the main() method
to use the default wsgiref server.

functions.name: Pyro4.utils.httpgateway.singlyfy_parameters
documentation:
Makes a cgi-parsed parameter dictionary into a dict where the values that
are just a list of a single value, are converted to just that single value.

modules.name: Pyro4.utils.flame
documentation:
Pyro FLAME:  Foreign Location Automatic Module Exposer.
Easy but potentially very dangerous way of exposing remote modules and builtins.
Flame requires the pickle serializer to be used.

Pyro - Python Remote Objects.  Copyright by Irmen de Jong (irmen@razorvine.net).

functions.name: Pyro4.utils.flame.createModule
documentation:
Utility function to create a new module with the given name (dotted notation allowed), directly from the source string.
Adds it to sys.modules, and returns the new module object.
If you provide a namespace dict (such as `globals()`), it will import the module into that namespace too.

functions.name: Pyro4.utils.flame.start
documentation:
Create and register a Flame server in the given daemon.
Be *very* cautious before starting this: it allows the clients full access to everything on your system.

functions.name: Pyro4.utils.flame.connect
documentation:
Connect to a Flame server on the given location, for instance localhost:9999 or ./u:unixsock
This is just a convenience function to creates an appropriate Pyro proxy.

classes.name: Pyro4.utils.flame.FlameModule
documentation: Proxy to a remote module.

classes.name: Pyro4.utils.flame.FlameBuiltin
documentation: Proxy to a remote builtin function.

classes.name: Pyro4.utils.flame.RemoteInteractiveConsole

inner_classes.name: Pyro4.utils.flame.RemoteInteractiveConsole.LineSendingConsole
documentation: makes sure the lines are sent to the remote console
Proxy to a remote interactive console.

classes.name: Pyro4.utils.flame.InteractiveConsole
documentation: Interactive console wrapper that saves output written to stdout so it can be returned as value

classes.name: Pyro4.utils.flame.Flame

methods.name: Pyro4.utils.flame.Flame.module
documentation:
Import a module on the server given by the module name and returns a proxy to it.
The returned proxy does not support direct attribute access, if you want that,
you should use the `evaluate` method instead.

methods.name: Pyro4.utils.flame.Flame.builtin
documentation: returns a proxy to the given builtin on the server

methods.name: Pyro4.utils.flame.Flame.execute
documentation: execute a piece of code

methods.name: Pyro4.utils.flame.Flame.evaluate
documentation: evaluate an expression and return its result

methods.name: Pyro4.utils.flame.Flame.sendmodule
documentation:
Send the source of a module to the server and make the server load it.
Note that you still have to actually `import` it on the server to access it.
Sending a module again will replace the previous one with the new.

methods.name: Pyro4.utils.flame.Flame.getmodule
documentation: obtain the source code from a module on the server

methods.name: Pyro4.utils.flame.Flame.sendfile
documentation: store a new file on the server

methods.name: Pyro4.utils.flame.Flame.getfile
documentation: read any accessible file from the server

methods.name: Pyro4.utils.flame.Flame.console
documentation: get a proxy for a remote interactive console session

The actual FLAME server logic.
Usually created by using {py:meth}`core.Daemon.startFlame`.
Be *very* cautious before starting this: it allows the clients full access to everything on your system.

modules.name: Pyro4.core
documentation:
Core logic (uri, daemon, proxy stuff).

Pyro - Python Remote Objects.  Copyright by Irmen de Jong (irmen@razorvine.net).

functions.name: Pyro4.core.batch
documentation: convenience method to get a batch proxy adapter

functions.name: Pyro4.core.asyncproxy
documentation: convenience method to set proxy to asynchronous or sync mode.

functions.name: Pyro4.core.pyroObjectToAutoProxy
documentation: reduce function that automatically replaces Pyro objects by a Proxy

functions.name: Pyro4.core.callback
documentation:
decorator to mark a method to be a 'callback'. This will make Pyro
raise any errors also on the callback side, and not only on the side
that does the callback call.
decorators

functions.name: Pyro4.core.oneway
documentation: decorator to mark a method to be oneway (client won't wait for a response)

functions.name: Pyro4.core.expose
documentation:
Decorator to mark a method or class to be exposed for remote calls (relevant when REQUIRE_EXPOSE=True)
You can apply it to a method or a class as a whole.
If you need to change the default instance mode or instance creator, also use a @behavior decorator.

functions.name: Pyro4.core.behavior
documentation: Decorator to specify the server behavior of your Pyro class.

functions.name: Pyro4.core._log_wiredata
documentation: logs all the given properties of the wire message in the given logger

functions.name: Pyro4.core._resolve
documentation:
Resolve a 'magic' uri (PYRONAME, PYROMETA) into the direct PYRO uri.
It finds a name server, and use that to resolve a PYRONAME uri into the direct PYRO uri pointing to the named object.
If uri is already a PYRO uri, it is returned unmodified.
You can consider this a shortcut function so that you don't have to locate and use a name server proxy yourself.
Note: if you need to resolve more than a few names, consider using the name server directly instead of repeatedly
calling this function, to avoid the name server lookup overhead from each call.
name server utility function, here to avoid cyclic dependencies

functions.name: Pyro4.core._locateNS
documentation: Get a proxy for a name server somewhere in the network.
name server utility function, here to avoid cyclic dependencies

functions.name: Pyro4.core.asyncproxy
documentation: convenience method to set proxy to asynchronous or sync mode.

variables.name: Pyro4.core.current_context
documentation: the context object for the current call. (thread-local)

classes.name: Pyro4.core.URI

methods.name: Pyro4.core.URI.isUnixsockLocation
documentation: determine if a location string is for a Unix domain socket

methods.name: Pyro4.core.URI.location
documentation: property containing the location string, for instance ``"servername.you.com:5555"``

methods.name: Pyro4.core.URI.asString
documentation: the string representation of this object

methods.name: Pyro4.core.URI.__getstate__
documentation: note: getstate/setstate are not needed if we use pickle protocol 2,
but this way it helps pickle to make the representation smaller by omitting all attribute names.

Pyro object URI (universal resource identifier).
The uri format is like this: `PYRO:objectid@location` where location is one of:

- `hostname:port` (tcp/ip socket on given port)
- `./u:sockname` (Unix domain socket on localhost)

There is also a 'Magic format' for simple name resolution using Name server:

: `PYRONAME:objectname[@location]`  (optional name server location, can also omit location port)

And one that looks up things in the name server by metadata:

: `PYROMETA:meta1,meta2,...[@location]`  (optional name server location, can also omit location port)

You can write the protocol in lowercase if you like (`pyro:...`) but it will
automatically be converted to uppercase internally.

classes.name: Pyro4.core._RemoteMethod
documentation: method call abstraction

classes.name: Pyro4.core.Proxy

methods.name: Pyro4.core.Proxy._pyroHmacKey
documentation: the HMAC key (bytes) that this proxy uses

methods.name: Pyro4.core.Proxy._pyroRelease
documentation: release the connection to the pyro daemon

methods.name: Pyro4.core.Proxy._pyroBind
documentation:
Bind this proxy to the exact object from the uri. That means that the proxy's uri
will be updated with a direct PYRO uri, if it isn't one yet.
If the proxy is already bound, it will not bind again.

methods.name: Pyro4.core.Proxy._pyroInvoke
documentation: perform the remote method call communication

methods.name: Pyro4.core.Proxy.__pyroCreateConnection
documentation:
Connects this proxy to the remote Pyro daemon. Does connection handshake.
Returns true if a new connection was made, false if an existing one was already present.

methods.name: Pyro4.core.Proxy._pyroGetMetadata
documentation:
Get metadata from server (methods, attrs, oneway, ...) and remember them in some attributes of the proxy.
Usually this will already be known due to the default behavior of the connect handshake, where the
connect response also includes the metadata.

methods.name: Pyro4.core.Proxy._pyroReconnect
documentation:
(Re)connect the proxy to the daemon containing the pyro object which the proxy is for.
In contrast to the _pyroBind method, this one first releases the connection (if the proxy is still connected)
and retries making a new connection until it succeeds or the given amount of tries ran out.

methods.name: Pyro4.core.Proxy._pyroBatch
documentation: returns a helper class that lets you create batched method calls on the proxy

methods.name: Pyro4.core.Proxy._pyroAsync
documentation:
turns the proxy into asynchronous mode so you can do asynchronous method calls,
or sets it back to normal sync mode if you set asynchronous=False.
This setting is strictly on a per-proxy basis (unless an exact clone is made
via copy.copy).

methods.name: Pyro4.core.Proxy._pyroAnnotations
documentation:
Override to return a dict with custom user annotations to be sent with each request message.
Code using Pyro 4.56 or newer can skip this and instead set the annotations directly on the context object.

methods.name: Pyro4.core.Proxy._pyroResponseAnnotations
documentation:
Process any response annotations (dictionary set by the daemon).
Usually this contains the internal Pyro annotations such as hmac and correlation id,
and if you override the annotations method in the daemon, can contain your own annotations as well.
Code using Pyro 4.56 or newer can skip this and instead read the response_annotations directly from the context object.

methods.name: Pyro4.core.Proxy._pyroValidateHandshake
documentation:
Process and validate the initial connection handshake response data received from the daemon.
Simply return without error if everything is ok.
Raise an exception if something is wrong and the connection should not be made.

methods.name: Pyro4.core.Proxy.__serializeBlobArgs
documentation:
Special handling of a "blob" argument that has to stay serialized until explicitly deserialized in client code.
This makes efficient, transparent gateways or dispatchers and such possible:
they don't have to de/reserialize the message and are independent from the serialized class definitions.
Annotations are passed in because some blob metadata is added. They're not part of the blob itself.

Pyro proxy for a remote object. Intercepts method calls and dispatches them to the remote object.

```
.. automethod:: _pyroBind
```

```
.. automethod:: _pyroRelease
```

```
.. automethod:: _pyroReconnect
```

```
.. automethod:: _pyroBatch
```

```
.. automethod:: _pyroAsync
```

```
.. automethod:: _pyroAnnotations
```

```
.. automethod:: _pyroResponseAnnotations
```

```
.. automethod:: _pyroValidateHandshake
```

```
.. autoattribute:: _pyroTimeout
```

```
.. autoattribute:: _pyroHmacKey
```

```
.. attribute:: _pyroMaxRetries

    Number of retries to perform on communication calls by this proxy, allows you to override the default setting.
```

```
.. attribute:: _pyroSerializer

    Name of the serializer to use by this proxy, allows you to override the default setting.
```

```
.. attribute:: _pyroHandshake

    The data object that should be sent in the initial connection handshake message. Can be any serializable object.
```

classes.name: Pyro4.core._StreamResultIterator
documentation:
Pyro returns this as a result of a remote call which returns an iterator or generator.
It is a normal iterable and produces elements on demand from the remote iterator.
You can simply use it in for loops, list comprehensions etc.

classes.name: Pyro4.core._BatchedRemoteMethod
documentation: method call abstraction that is used with batched calls

classes.name: Pyro4.core._BatchProxyAdapter
documentation:
Helper class that lets you batch multiple method calls into one.
It is constructed with a reference to the normal proxy that will
carry out the batched calls. Call methods on this object that you want to batch,
and finally call the batch proxy itself. That call will return a generator
for the results of every method call in the batch (in sequence).

classes.name: Pyro4.core._AsyncRemoteMethod
documentation: asynchronous method call abstraction (call will run in a background thread)

classes.name: Pyro4.core.DaemonObject

methods.name: Pyro4.core.DaemonObject.registered
documentation: returns a list of all object names registered in this daemon

methods.name: Pyro4.core.DaemonObject.ping
documentation: a simple do-nothing method for testing purposes

methods.name: Pyro4.core.DaemonObject.info
documentation: return some descriptive information about the daemon

methods.name: Pyro4.core.DaemonObject.get_metadata
documentation:
Get metadata for the given object (exposed methods, oneways, attributes).
If you get an error in your proxy saying that 'DaemonObject' has no attribute 'get_metadata',
you're probably connecting to an older Pyro version (4.26 or earlier).
Either upgrade the Pyro version or set METADATA config item to False in your client code.
The part of the daemon that is exposed as a Pyro object.

classes.name: Pyro4.core.Daemon

methods.name: Pyro4.core.Daemon.sock
documentation: the server socket used by the daemon

methods.name: Pyro4.core.Daemon.sockets
documentation: list of all sockets used by the daemon (server socket and all active client sockets)

methods.name: Pyro4.core.Daemon.selector
documentation: the multiplexing selector used, if using the multiplex server type

methods.name: Pyro4.core.Daemon.serveSimple
documentation:
Basic method to fire up a daemon (or supply one yourself).
objects is a dict containing objects to register as keys, and
their names (or None) as values. If ns is true they will be registered
in the naming server as well, otherwise they just stay local.
If you need to publish on a unix domain socket you can't use this shortcut method.
See the documentation on 'publishing objects' (in chapter: Servers) for more details.

methods.name: Pyro4.core.Daemon.requestLoop
documentation:
Goes in a loop to service incoming requests, until someone breaks this
or calls shutdown from another thread.

methods.name: Pyro4.core.Daemon.events
documentation: for use in an external event loop: handle any requests that are pending for this daemon

methods.name: Pyro4.core.Daemon.shutdown
documentation: Cleanly terminate a daemon that is running in the requestloop.

methods.name: Pyro4.core.Daemon._handshake
documentation:
Perform connection handshake with new clients.
Client sends a MSG_CONNECT message with a serialized data payload.
If all is well, return with a CONNECT_OK message.
The reason we're not doing this with a MSG_INVOKE method call on the daemon
(like when retrieving the metadata) is because we need to force the clients
to get past an initial connect handshake before letting them invoke any method.
Return True for successful handshake, False if something was wrong.
If a denied_reason is given, the handshake will fail with the given reason.

methods.name: Pyro4.core.Daemon.validateHandshake
documentation:
Override this to create a connection validator for new client connections.
It should return a response data object normally if the connection is okay,
or should raise an exception if the connection should be denied.

methods.name: Pyro4.core.Daemon.clientDisconnect
documentation:
Override this to handle a client disconnect.
Conn is the SocketConnection object that was disconnected.

methods.name: Pyro4.core.Daemon.handleRequest
documentation:
Handle incoming Pyro request. Catches any exception that may occur and
wraps it in a reply to the calling side, as to not make this server side loop
terminate due to exceptions caused by remote invocations.

methods.name: Pyro4.core.Daemon._housekeeping
documentation: Perform periodical housekeeping actions (cleanups etc)

methods.name: Pyro4.core.Daemon.housekeeping
documentation:
Override this to add custom periodic housekeeping (cleanup) logic.
This will be called every few seconds by the running daemon's request loop.

methods.name: Pyro4.core.Daemon._getInstance
documentation: Find or create a new instance of the class

methods.name: Pyro4.core.Daemon._sendExceptionResponse
documentation: send an exception back including the local traceback info

methods.name: Pyro4.core.Daemon.register
documentation:
Register a Pyro object under the given id. Note that this object is now only
known inside this daemon, it is not automatically available in a name server.
This method returns a URI for the registered object.
Pyro checks if an object is already registered, unless you set force=True.
You can register a class or an object (instance) directly.
For a class, Pyro will create instances of it to handle the remote calls according
to the instance_mode (set via @expose on the class). The default there is one object
per session (=proxy connection). If you register an object directly, Pyro will use
that single object for *all* remote calls.

methods.name: Pyro4.core.Daemon.unregister
documentation:
Remove a class or object from the known objects inside this daemon.
You can unregister the class/object directly, or with its id.

methods.name: Pyro4.core.Daemon.uriFor
documentation:
Get a URI for the given object (or object id) from this daemon.
Only a daemon can hand out proper uris because the access location is
contained in them.
Note that unregistered objects cannot be given an uri, but unregistered
object names can (it's just a string we're creating in that case).
If nat is set to False, the configured NAT address (if any) is ignored and it will
return an URI for the internal address.

methods.name: Pyro4.core.Daemon.resetMetadataCache
documentation:
Reset cache of metadata when a Daemon has available methods/attributes
dynamically updated.  Clients will have to get a new proxy to see changes

methods.name: Pyro4.core.Daemon.proxyFor
documentation:
Get a fully initialized Pyro Proxy for the given object (or object id) for this daemon.
If nat is False, the configured NAT address (if any) is ignored.
The object or id must be registered in this daemon, or you'll get an exception.
(you can't get a proxy for an unknown object)

methods.name: Pyro4.core.Daemon.close
documentation: Close down the server and release resources

methods.name: Pyro4.core.Daemon.annotations
documentation: Override to return a dict with custom user annotations to be sent with each response message.

methods.name: Pyro4.core.Daemon.combine
documentation:
Combines the event loop of the other daemon in the current daemon's loop.
You can then simply run the current daemon's requestLoop to serve both daemons.
This works fine on the multiplex server type, but doesn't work with the threaded server type.

Pyro daemon. Contains server side logic and dispatches incoming remote method calls
to the appropriate objects.

methods.name: Pyro4.core._CallContext.track_resource
documentation: keep a weak reference to the resource to be tracked for this connection

methods.name: Pyro4.core._CallContext.untrack_resource
documentation: no longer track the resource for this connection

classes.name: Pyro4.core.SerializedBlob

methods.name: Pyro4.core.SerializedBlob.deserialized
documentation: Retrieves the client data stored in this blob. Deserializes the data automatically if required.

Used to wrap some data to make Pyro pass this object transparently (it keeps the serialized payload as-is)
Only when you need to access the actual client data you can deserialize on demand.
This makes efficient, transparent gateways or dispatchers and such possible:
they don't have to de/reserialize the message and are independent from the serialized class definitions.
You have to pass this as the only parameter to a remote method call for Pyro to understand it.
Init arguments:
`info` = some (small) descriptive data about the blob. Can be a simple id or name or guid. Must be marshallable.
`data` = the actual client data payload that you want to transfer in the blob. Can be anything that you would
otherwise have used as regular remote call arguments.
